attributes.stem: A 28-year-old woman presents to the antenatal clinic for her first appointment. She is 12 weeks pregnant with monochorionic diamniotic (MCDA) twins, confirmed on a dating scan. She has no significant medical history and does not take any regular medications. This is her first pregnancy. On examination, she appears well with vital signs within normal limits. The obstetrician discusses the management plan for her pregnancy.
attributes.stemQuestion: Which one of the following is the most appropriate ultrasound screening schedule for twin-to-twin transfusion syndrome (TTTS) in this pregnancy?
options: Fortnightly scans from 24 weeks gestation until delivery
Monthly scans from 16 weeks gestation until delivery
Single detailed scan at 20 weeks gestation
Fortnightly scans from 16 weeks gestation until delivery
Weekly scans from 16 weeks gestation until delivery
attributes.explanation:
This patient is pregnant with monochorionic diamniotic (MCDA) twins, which are at risk of developing twin-to-twin transfusion syndrome (TTTS). TTTS is a serious complication that can occur in about 10-15% of MCDA twin pregnancies. The most appropriate screening schedule for TTTS in this case is fortnightly scans from 16 weeks gestation until delivery.

Weekly scans from 16 weeks gestation until delivery would be too frequent and not supported by current evidence. While it might detect changes earlier, it would increase patient anxiety and healthcare costs without clear benefit.

Monthly scans from 16 weeks gestation until delivery would be too infrequent and might miss the opportunity for early intervention if TTTS develops. The interval between scans should not exceed two weeks throughout the pregnancy.

A single detailed scan at 20 weeks gestation is insufficient for TTTS screening. This approach would miss cases that develop before or after 20 weeks and does not allow for the close monitoring required in MCDA twin pregnancies.

Fortnightly scans from 24 weeks gestation until delivery misses the critical early period for TTTS development. While ongoing monitoring is important throughout the pregnancy, the highest risk period for TTTS begins at 16 weeks gestation.

The recommended screening schedule (fortnightly scans from 16 weeks gestation until delivery) allows for early detection of TTTS, which is crucial for timely intervention and improved outcomes. During these scans, clinicians assess for signs of TTTS such as discordant amniotic fluid volumes, abnormal Doppler studies, and size discrepancies between the twins. This frequent monitoring throughout the pregnancy ensures that any complications, including but not limited to TTTS, can be detected and managed promptly.
learningPoints: Monochorionic diamniotic (MCDA) twins are at risk of twin-to-twin transfusion syndrome (TTTS).
Regular ultrasound screening for TTTS in MCDA twins should occur every two weeks from 16 weeks gestation until delivery.
Early detection of TTTS through ultrasound screening allows for timely intervention and improved outcomes.
references: Borse V, Shanks AL. Twin-to-Twin Transfusion Syndrome. In: StatPearls [Internet]. Treasure Island (FL): StatPearls Publishing; 2022 [updated 2022 Oct 10]. Available from: https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/books/NBK532250/

International Society of Ultrasound in Obstetrics and Gynecology (ISUOG). ISUOG Practice Guidelines: Role of ultrasound in twin pregnancy. Ultrasound Obstet Gynecol. 2016. Available from: https://www.isuog.org/static/6096a7c8-4408-4c73-b4b6a8bf1466229a/ISUOG-Practice-Guidelines-ultrasound-twin-pregnancy.pdf
